nativeRange.habitat: Turbid rivers, tributaries, and seasonally flooded areas; shelters during daylight in tangles of submerged roots, hollow or rotting tree stumps, and aquatic vegetation, then emerges to feed in open water after dark

decorPreferences: driftwood tangles and hollow logs providing caves and crevices
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias) to diffuse light
PVC or bamboo tubes as bolt-holes

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild, feeding on insects and their larvae. In the aquarium, offer live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, mysis, and small earthworm pieces at lights-out. Most individuals learn to accept sinking carnivore pellets and granules over time. Feed after lights-off to match their nocturnal rhythm.

goodWith: similarly sized peaceful South American species
avoidWith: very small fish (nano tetras, small rasboras) — may be consumed nocturnally

summary: A sleek, dark-bodied catfish from the Amazon basin, speckled with white spots, that hides in wood tangles by day and emerges to feed after dark. Like all members of its family, it uses internal fertilisation — an unusual trait among catfishes — and females can store sperm to produce multiple clutches. Like all scaleless fish, it is sensitive to copper-based medications.
careNotes:
The Midnight Catfish is a strictly nocturnal Auchenipteridae native to the upper and central Amazon basin of Peru, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and the Essequibo drainage of Guyana. Adults typically reach 9–12 cm. The body is dark blackish-brown with scattered white or cream spots, and the coracoid bone (a skeletal element of the pectoral girdle) is visible through the thin skin of the belly — the anatomical feature that gives the species its Latin name. Because these fish are scaleless, they are sensitive to copper-based medications; always use copper-free treatments.

A tank of at least 80 litres with an 80 cm footprint satisfies a small group of three. Provide deep tangles of driftwood, hollow logs, bamboo tubes, and dense low-light plants (Anubias, Java fern) to replicate the shaded root-zone habitat. Smooth sand substrate is preferred over gravel. Lighting should be dim or interrupted by floating cover; bright light suppresses natural behaviour. Moderate, gentle filtration — aiming for around four to five times the tank volume per hour — keeps water clean without generating a strong current.

Water should be soft and slightly acidic: temperature 22–26 °C, pH 6.0–7.2, gH 0–10, kH 0–6. The species tolerates slightly wider values once established but does not adapt well to hard alkaline conditions.

These catfish are peaceful toward tankmates of comparable size but will prey on very small fish — nano tetras, fry, dwarf shrimp — after lights-off. Best combined with medium characins, dwarf cichlids, corydoras, and bristlenose plecos. They are non-aggressive toward conspecifics and are best kept in groups of three or more; solitary individuals tend to remain permanently hidden.

Feed exclusively after lights-out: bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, mysis, or small earthworm sections. Most individuals transition to quality sinking pellets or granules over weeks; target-feeding with a turkey baster near hiding spots accelerates acceptance.
breedingNotes: Internal fertilisation via the male’s modified anal fin — which works similarly to the gonopodium (a specialised reproductive fin) found in livebearing fish — is shared across the driftwood catfish family and is rare among catfishes generally. After mating, females store sperm and can produce multiple clutches without repeated access to a male. Eggs are deposited on the substrate or among vegetation; no parental care is provided. Fry become free-swimming after approximately 7–10 days and initially accept baby brine shrimp and microworm. Breeding in aquaria has been documented but is uncommon; the trigger conditions are not fully characterised.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Auchenipterichthys coracoideus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size 90–110 mm, temperature 22–25 °C, pH 6.0–7.2, hardness 0–179 ppm, minimum tank base 80 × 30 cm, native range (upper/central Peruvian/Brazilian Amazon plus Essequibo drainage), diet (insects and larvae; frozen/live foods at lights-out), temperament (peaceful, nocturnal), internal fertilisation via modified anal fin, and sperm-storage breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Auchenipterichthys coracoideus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Siluriformes, Auchenipteridae), native range (upper Amazon River basin, South America), trophic level 3.4 (carnivorous), IUCN Least Concern status. FishBase lists max SL 19.2 cm which likely conflates closely related congeners; aquarium-realistic size of 9–12 cm confirmed by SeriouslyFish and fishkeeper.co.uk.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Zamora Woodcat
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~12 cm, temperature 22–26 °C, native range (Brazil, Ecuador, Colombia, Peru), diet preferences (bloodworm, mysis, krill, pellets), group-keeping recommendation (5+), fry free-swimming in 7–10 days on baby brine shrimp and microworm.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Auchenipterichthys coracoideus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy, nocturnal behaviour, IUCN Least Concern, and common names (zamora woodcat, midnight catfish). Cross-reference only.

fish.breedingNotes: Fertilisation is internal, achieved via the male's modified anal fin. Females can store sperm and produce multiple clutches without repeated access to a male. Eggs are deposited on the substrate and receive no parental care. Fry become free-swimming after approximately 7–10 days. Breeding has been documented in home aquaria but remains uncommon.
